data.content: The bug is on line 29 — `ProcessPayment` only checks for zero, not negative amounts. The fastest workflow is `dotnet watch test`, which auto-reruns tests on every file save. Let me set it up:

toolRequests.name: bash

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolRequests.intentionSummary: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.reasoningText:
The bug is clear: `ProcessPayment` only checks for `amount == 0` but doesn't check for negative amounts. The test on line 24 expects an `ArgumentException` for `-50.00m`, but the method just returns `true`.

The user wants the fastest iteration workflow. The answer is `dotnet watch test` which will re-run tests automatically on file save. Let me start that up, then fix the bug.

arguments.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

toolCalls.name: bash
toolCalls.args: {"command": "cd /tmp/sv-437ceb6b4a4a49ecbdc10ec3dc991a9c && dotnet watch test --filter \"ProcessPayment_NegativeAmount_ThrowsException\" 2>&1", "description": "Start dotnet watch test for the failing test", "mode": "async", "shellId": "watch-test"}

toolArgs.description: Start dotnet watch test for the failing test

data.content: Missing `using System;`. Let me fix both the missing import and the actual bug — watch will auto-rerun:

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");

data.reasoningText: There's a missing `using System;` directive. Let me fix that and the actual bug at the same time. Since `dotnet watch` is running, the tests will automatically re-run after I save.

arguments.old_str:
if (amount == 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount cannot be zero");
        // Missing: validation for negative amounts
arguments.new_str: if (amount <= 0) throw new ArgumentException("Amount must be positive");
